nativeRange.habitat: Upper edges of steep outer reef slopes with moderate currents; forms large mixed-sex aggregations in open water above coral.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical reef-associated planktivore zone above outer-reef drop-offs.

decorPreferences: open swimming space above live rock

diet.notes: In the wild feeds on zooplankton in the open water column. In captivity is generally advised to receive 3–5 small feedings per day of mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and high-quality pellet/flake foods. A refugium that exports copepods and amphipods can help supplement steady grazing. Newly introduced specimens may initially refuse prepared foods and tend to do best when offered varied frozen items.

summary: A peaceful planktivorous anthias generally advised for medium-to-large reef tanks; males display bright red dorsal fins and females are yellowish-orange. Requires multiple small daily feedings and a tightly-sealed lid.
careNotes: Dispar Anthias are open-water planktivores that have been documented thriving with ample horizontal swimming space above the rockwork; a tank length of about 120 cm or more is generally advised. They are constant grazers and tend to decline rapidly without 3–5 small feedings per day, ideally of varied frozen mysis, brine, and finely chopped seafoods. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite: all individuals begin as females, and the dominant female of a harem will transition into a male when no male is present. Best kept singly, as a single male with a harem of 4–10 females, or as a small group of all females in suitably sized tanks; multiple males are commonly considered incompatible due to territorial conflict. Reports of jumping are common, so a tightly-sealed lid is strongly advised. Generally considered reef-safe with corals and ornamental invertebrates. Newly imported specimens have been known to refuse prepared foods initially and may need live or heavily enriched frozen offerings before accepting a wider diet. FishBase recently reclassified this species into the genus Nemanthias (as Nemanthias dispar), though it remains widely sold under Pseudanthias dispar in the trade.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has rarely been reported; pelagic spawners that broadcast eggs into open water. Larvae are difficult to rear. Sex change from female to male occurs naturally when the dominant male is removed from a harem.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary trade source for sizing, water parameters, temperament, and reef-safety. States 70 gal minimum (single) / 125 gal for harem, 4 in max size, 72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, peaceful, reef-safe, carnivore.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (now Nemanthias dispar, family Anthiadidae), max length 9.5 cm TL, depth 0-18 m typically 1-15 m, distribution from Christmas Island to Line Islands south to Great Barrier Reef, Fiji and Samoa. Trophic level 3.3.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (300 L minimum, ~26 C, SG ~1.026), reef-safe status, jumping risk (known to jump out of open aquaria), and harem behavior (8+ recommended to reduce subordinate stress). Confirmed multiple-daily-feeding requirement.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner; larvae rarely reared in captivity.
